chunk_summary: Guidelines for texture progression and feeding safety in preterm/LBW/ELBW infants emphasize starting with thick purees, advancing textures gradually, and monitoring for safety concerns like choking or aspiration, while ensuring complementary feeding supports milk intake.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of texture progression in preterm/LBW/ELBW infants to support oral-motor development and feeding safety.
Learn the recommended timeline for advancing complementary feeding textures from purees to finger foods.
Identify safety precautions and signs of feeding difficulties, such as choking, aspiration, or stress cues.
Recognize the role of complementary feeding in supporting, not replacing, breast milk or prescribed milk volumes.
key_facts: Start with thick purees and pastes, progressing to mashed foods with soft lumps over 3–6 weeks.
Introduce soft finger foods and lumpier textures by 7–9 months corrected age if the infant can sit with support and has stable respiratory status.
Avoid advancing textures in infants with unresolved cough/choke during milk feeds, frequent desaturations, or suspected aspiration.
Maintain upright positioning, slow spoon delivery, and stop feeding with stress cues like arching or breath-holding.
Complementary feeding should not replace breast milk or prescribed milk volumes in the early months.
tags_mesh: Feeding and Eating Disorders of Childhood
reference_quote: Advancing texture too slowly can prolong feeding aversion, while advancing too fast can increase choking or aspiration risk. (Source: https://www.who.int/publications/i/item/9789240081864)
document_key_topics: Feeding safety and aspiration risk management
Role of complementary feeding in preterm nutrition
